evaluation_inputs.system_description: A centralized Data Center system designed to integrate dozens of existing, disparate academic information systems at Universitas Pendidikan Ganesha. The system functions as a backbone Data Warehouse (DWH) to synchronize data across the university, reduce redundancy, and support institutional accreditation. It aggregates data from various web-based applications (mostly PHP/MySQL) and was evaluated across three architectural options (Direct Connection, Web Services, ESB) before settling on a Spring Framework-based solution to accommodate legacy constraints.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Low maturity; the system was based on an initial prototype with no standard architectural evaluation practice previously applied. Design decisions were initially driven by immediate needs under tight schedules without thorough analysis.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: How can existing systems be integrated to minimize data duplication?
What are the available architectural options considering the low-cost and open-source constraints?
How can binary data (images, audio, video) be handled if existing databases only store file paths?

hardware_view_description: Dozens of existing information systems operating on separate servers with separate databases.
Most existing applications are web-based, coded in PHP, using MySQL instances.
Binary files (images/audio) are stored physically on server hard drives, not in the databases.
runtime_constraints: Strict reliance on low-cost, open-source software.
Limited human resources and developer expertise (mostly fresh graduates).
Legacy systems cannot accept major behavioral changes or new adapter installations.

quantitative_model_parameters.network_protocol: HTTP/REST (for Web Services option), JDBC (for Direct Connection)

questions: Does the ESB support binary data transfer natively?
Can legacy systems connect to the ESB without installing new adapters?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: All applications need to be connected to each other and authenticated such that to avoid any data redundancy among them.
Each of the other connected applications must be able to connect to a centralized data-warehouse and pull data upon request as needed.
The DC system must be able to sync any duplicated master data from different applications.
The DC system must be able to verify the identity of the application that connects to it before an access is allowed.
The source system should not experience overload when another system is connected to get data.
Add more data structures into the existing connected application and reflect the changes into the data-warehouse.
The DC system must facilitate any format of data transfer including binary format such as images, audio and video files.
Small to no change should be needed in any of the connected applications in order to implement the solution.
The cost in term of the required software development has to be minimum and preferably using open-source software.
The developed system has to be developer-friendly for enhancement purposes.

business_goals: Organize and synchronize information from existing systems
Reduce duplication issues and problems from various data structures
Create a centralized source for overall academic activity
technical_constraints: Existing systems use PHP and MySQL
Existing systems are disparate and separate

architectural_approaches.description: A centralized Data Center acting as a backbone DWH to aggregate and synchronize data from disparate academic systems

addressed_attributes.rationale: Centralized data supports institutional accreditation requirements
Reduces data duplication and provides single source of truth

architectural_approaches.description: Direct JDBC connections between systems and data warehouse
ESB-based integration with message routing and transformation
Spring Framework-based implementation to accommodate legacy constraints with open-source technology

children.refinement: Authentication of connected applications before data access
children.scenario_description: The DC system must verify the identity of any application attempting to connect and access data, ensuring 100% of data access requests are authenticated before granting access

children.refinement: Prevention of unauthorized data access and data redundancy across systems
children.scenario_description: All applications connected to the centralized DWH must be authenticated to prevent unauthorized access and eliminate data redundancy among disparate academic systems

children.refinement: Secure credential management for legacy system connections
children.scenario_description: Spring Framework must securely store and manage JDBC credentials for connections to legacy academic systems without exposing sensitive authentication data

children.refinement: Source system availability during data synchronization (CPU < 70%)
children.scenario_description: When connected systems pull data from source applications, source system CPU utilization must remain below 70% to prevent operational overload
The DC system must synchronize duplicated master data from different applications and propagate changes to the DWH within 5 minutes without duplication errors
Connected applications must be able to pull data from the centralized DWH upon request with acceptable response times during normal operations
The Spring Framework-based DWH must handle concurrent data synchronization operations from multiple disparate academic systems without performance degradation

children.refinement: DWH query response time under reporting load (< 3 seconds)
children.scenario_description: Analytical queries against the centralized DWH must return results within 3 seconds under typical reporting workload to support institutional accreditation processes

children.refinement: Minimal changes to existing legacy applications (< 100 LOC)
children.scenario_description: Implementing the Spring Framework integration solution must require fewer than 100 lines of code changes in any connected legacy application

children.refinement: Addition of new data structures to existing applications
children.scenario_description: New data structures added to connected applications must be automatically reflected in the DWH schema without major refactoring of the Spring Framework integration layer
Developers with limited Spring Framework experience must be able to add new integration features or modify existing data mappings within 2 weeks

children.refinement: Addition of new legacy system to DWH integration
children.scenario_description: A new disparate academic system must be integrated into the centralized DWH using the Spring Framework architecture within 3 weeks without impacting existing integrations

children.refinement: Use of open-source components for new integrations
children.scenario_description: New integration connectors must be developed using only open-source libraries compatible with the Spring Framework to minimize licensing costs

children.refinement: Support for institutional accreditation data requirements
children.scenario_description: The centralized DWH must provide aggregated data from all academic systems in formats required for institutional accreditation processes

children.refinement: Binary data format support (images, audio, video)
children.scenario_description: The DC system must facilitate transfer and storage of binary format data including images, audio, and video files stored on legacy system hard drives

scenarios.scenario_text: The DC system must verify the identity of any application attempting to connect and access data, ensuring 100% of data access requests are authenticated before granting access
All applications connected to the centralized DWH must be authenticated to prevent unauthorized access and eliminate data redundancy among disparate academic systems
Spring Framework must securely store and manage JDBC credentials for connections to legacy academic systems without exposing sensitive authentication data
When connected systems pull data from source applications, source system CPU utilization must remain below 70% to prevent operational overload
The DC system must synchronize duplicated master data from different applications and propagate changes to the DWH within 5 minutes without duplication errors
Connected applications must be able to pull data from the centralized DWH upon request with acceptable response times during normal operations
The Spring Framework-based DWH must handle concurrent data synchronization operations from multiple disparate academic systems without performance degradation
Analytical queries against the centralized DWH must return results within 3 seconds under typical reporting workload to support institutional accreditation processes

scenario_reference.text: The DC system must verify the identity of any application attempting to connect and access data, ensuring 100% of data access requests are authenticated before granting access

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with multiple applications requesting data access
analysis_records.stimulus: Application attempts to connect and access DWH data
analysis_records.response_measure: 100% of data access requests authenticated before granting access
architectural_decisions: Spring Security framework for authentication layer
Token-based authentication for application identity verification
Centralized authentication service at DWH entry point
analysis_records.reasoning: Spring Security provides robust authentication mechanisms. By implementing token-based authentication at the DWH gateway, all incoming requests can be intercepted and validated before data access is granted. This ensures 100% coverage as no direct database access bypasses the authentication layer.

risks.description: Authentication service becomes single point of failure
risks.rationale: If the centralized authentication service fails, all data access requests will be blocked, preventing legitimate applications from accessing the DWH

risks.description: Performance overhead from authentication on every request
risks.rationale: Validating authentication tokens on every data access request adds latency, potentially conflicting with performance scenarios P-02 and P-03

non_risks.rationale: Spring Security is a well-established, battle-tested framework with proven authentication capabilities, reducing implementation risk

conflicting_attributes: Performance (positive: reduces authentication overhead)
Security (negative: increases window of vulnerability if token is compromised)

scenario_reference.text: Spring Framework must securely store and manage JDBC credentials for connections to legacy academic systems without exposing sensitive authentication data

analysis_records.environment: Spring Framework connecting to multiple legacy systems with different credentials
analysis_records.stimulus: System initialization or connection pool creation requiring database credentials
analysis_records.response_measure: Zero exposure of credentials in logs, configuration files, or memory dumps
architectural_decisions: Spring Cloud Config with encryption for credential storage
Environment variable injection for sensitive properties
Vault integration for credential management
Connection pooling with encrypted credential storage
analysis_records.reasoning: Spring Framework supports externalized configuration with encryption capabilities. By using Spring Cloud Config or HashiCorp Vault integration, credentials can be encrypted at rest and decrypted only at runtime. Connection pools maintain credentials in memory securely without logging or exposing them in stack traces.

risks.description: Credential exposure in application logs during debugging
risks.rationale: Developers may inadvertently log connection strings or enable verbose logging that exposes credentials during troubleshooting

risks.description: Hardcoded credentials in legacy application code
risks.rationale: If legacy systems already have hardcoded credentials, the Spring integration layer inherits this vulnerability when connecting to them

non_risks.rationale: Spring provides built-in support for encrypted properties and secure credential management through multiple mechanisms

tradeoff_points.parameter: Credential storage location (local vs. remote vault)
conflicting_attributes: Security (positive: remote vault provides better security)
Performance (negative: remote vault adds network latency to connection initialization)

scenario_reference.text: When connected systems pull data from source applications, source system CPU utilization must remain below 70% to prevent operational overload

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with multiple systems synchronizing data from source applications
analysis_records.stimulus: Data synchronization request from DWH to source legacy system
architectural_decisions: Batch processing with configurable batch sizes
Rate limiting on data pull requests
Off-peak scheduling for large data transfers
Connection pooling to limit concurrent connections
Incremental data synchronization (delta only)
analysis_records.reasoning: By implementing rate limiting and batch size controls in the Spring integration layer, the load on source systems can be regulated. Scheduling large transfers during off-peak hours and using incremental synchronization reduces CPU impact. Connection pooling prevents connection storms that could spike CPU usage.

risks.description: Unpredictable query complexity from legacy systems
risks.rationale: Legacy systems may have inefficient database schemas or missing indexes, causing even small data pulls to consume excessive CPU

risks.description: Concurrent synchronization from multiple DWH instances
risks.rationale: If multiple DWH nodes or processes simultaneously pull data from the same source system, aggregate CPU load could exceed 70% threshold

risks.description: Lack of monitoring on source system CPU utilization
risks.rationale: Without real-time monitoring, the Spring Framework cannot dynamically adjust batch sizes or throttle requests when CPU approaches 70%

non_risks.rationale: Spring Batch provides robust batch processing with configurable chunk sizes and throttling, enabling controlled data extraction

conflicting_attributes: Performance - Source CPU (positive: smaller batches reduce CPU load)
Performance - Synchronization latency (negative: smaller batches increase total synchronization time)

scenario_reference.text: The DC system must synchronize duplicated master data from different applications and propagate changes to the DWH within 5 minutes without duplication errors

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with master data changes occurring in disparate source systems
analysis_records.stimulus: Master data update in any connected legacy application
analysis_records.response_measure: Changes propagated to DWH within 5 minutes with zero duplication errors
architectural_decisions: Change Data Capture (CDC) mechanism for detecting updates
Spring Integration message channels for event-driven synchronization
Master Data Management (MDM) logic for deduplication
Unique key resolution strategy across systems
Asynchronous processing with message queues
analysis_records.reasoning: Spring Integration provides event-driven architecture capabilities. By implementing CDC or polling mechanisms with 1-2 minute intervals, changes can be detected quickly. MDM logic in the Spring layer resolves duplicates using business keys before writing to DWH. Asynchronous processing ensures non-blocking propagation within the 5-minute window.

risks.description: Inconsistent master data keys across legacy systems
risks.rationale: Different legacy systems may use different identifiers for the same entity, making deduplication logic complex and error-prone

risks.description: Network latency between source systems and DWH
risks.rationale: If source systems are geographically distributed, network latency could consume significant portion of the 5-minute budget

risks.description: Concurrent updates to same master data entity
risks.rationale: If two systems update the same entity simultaneously, conflict resolution logic may introduce delays or errors

non_risks.rationale: Spring Integration provides robust message-driven architecture for near-real-time data propagation

conflicting_attributes: Performance - Synchronization latency (positive: higher frequency reduces latency)
Performance - Source system CPU (negative: higher frequency increases CPU load per P-01)

scenario_reference.text: The Spring Framework-based DWH must handle concurrent data synchronization operations from multiple disparate academic systems without performance degradation

analysis_records.environment: Peak operations with multiple legacy systems simultaneously synchronizing data
analysis_records.stimulus: Concurrent synchronization requests from 5+ disparate academic systems
analysis_records.response_measure: No performance degradation (throughput and latency remain within acceptable bounds)
architectural_decisions: Thread pool configuration for concurrent processing
Separate connection pools per source system
Asynchronous processing with Spring @Async
Database connection pooling (HikariCP)
Load balancing across multiple DWH nodes if needed
analysis_records.reasoning: Spring Framework supports concurrent processing through thread pools and asynchronous execution. By configuring separate connection pools for each source system and using HikariCP for efficient connection management, the system can handle multiple concurrent synchronizations. Thread pool sizing based on available CPU cores prevents resource contention.

risks.description: Thread pool exhaustion under high concurrency
risks.rationale: If more systems attempt synchronization than available threads, requests will queue and latency will increase

risks.description: Database connection pool saturation
risks.rationale: Concurrent synchronizations may exhaust available database connections to the DWH, causing blocking and timeouts

risks.description: Memory pressure from concurrent large data transfers
risks.rationale: If multiple systems transfer large datasets simultaneously, heap memory may be exhausted, causing garbage collection pauses or OutOfMemory errors

non_risks.rationale: Spring's @Async and TaskExecutor abstractions provide proven concurrent processing capabilities

sensitivity_points.parameter: Thread pool core and max size

conflicting_attributes: Performance - Concurrency (positive: larger pool handles more concurrent requests)
Performance - CPU utilization (negative: too many threads cause context switching overhead)

tradeoff_points.parameter: Connection pool size per source system
conflicting_attributes: Performance - DWH throughput (positive: more connections increase throughput)
Performance - Source system CPU (negative: more connections increase load per P-01)

scenario_reference.text: Implementing the Spring Framework integration solution must require fewer than 100 lines of code changes in any connected legacy application

analysis_records.environment: Integration of a legacy academic system with the Spring-based DWH
analysis_records.stimulus: Developer implements integration connector for a legacy system
analysis_records.response_measure: < 100 lines of code (LOC) changes in legacy application
architectural_decisions: Database-level integration using triggers or views
Spring Framework handles all integration logic externally
Configuration-driven integration (no code changes in legacy apps)
JDBC read-only access to legacy databases
Minimal instrumentation in legacy apps (optional event publishing)
analysis_records.reasoning: By implementing integration at the database level (reading from legacy databases via JDBC) or using database triggers to publish events, the Spring Framework can extract data without modifying legacy application code. Configuration files in Spring define data mappings and synchronization rules, keeping legacy apps unchanged.

risks.description: Complex data transformations require legacy app changes
risks.rationale: If legacy data formats are incompatible with DWH schema and cannot be transformed externally, legacy application code may need modification beyond 100 LOC

risks.description: Binary data access requires file system integration
risks.rationale: Per scenario C-02, binary files stored on legacy hard drives may require file system agents or APIs in legacy apps, potentially exceeding 100 LOC

risks.description: Real-time event publishing requires instrumentation
risks.rationale: If near-real-time synchronization (P-02) requires event publishing from legacy apps, instrumentation code may exceed 100 LOC

non_risks.rationale: JDBC-based data extraction requires zero changes to legacy application code, only database access configuration

sensitivity_points.parameter: Degree of schema mismatch between legacy and DWH

conflicting_attributes: Modifiability - Legacy app changes (positive: database-level requires zero LOC)
Performance - Synchronization latency (negative: database polling adds latency vs. real-time events)

scenario_reference.text: New data structures added to connected applications must be automatically reflected in the DWH schema without major refactoring of the Spring Framework integration layer

analysis_records.environment: Legacy application undergoes schema evolution (new tables/columns added)
analysis_records.stimulus: Developer adds new data structure to a connected legacy application
analysis_records.response_measure: DWH schema updated automatically without major Spring Framework refactoring
architectural_decisions: Schema discovery and introspection mechanisms
Dynamic mapping configuration (e.g., Hibernate auto-DDL)
Metadata-driven integration approach
Convention-over-configuration for new entities
Automated schema migration tools (Flyway/Liquibase)
analysis_records.reasoning: Spring Data JPA with Hibernate can auto-generate DWH schema based on entity definitions. By using schema introspection on legacy databases and metadata-driven mapping, new structures can be discovered and mapped automatically. Flyway or Liquibase can manage DWH schema migrations declaratively without code changes.

risks.description: Complex data types require custom mapping logic
risks.rationale: New data structures with complex types (nested objects, arrays) may require custom converters or transformers, necessitating Spring code changes

risks.description: Breaking schema changes in legacy systems
risks.rationale: If legacy apps rename or drop columns, automated mapping may fail or create data inconsistencies in DWH

risks.description: Performance impact of auto-DDL in production
risks.rationale: Hibernate auto-DDL can cause schema locks or performance issues in production DWH environments

non_risks.description: Spring Data JPA schema generation capabilities
non_risks.rationale: Spring Data JPA with Hibernate provides robust auto-DDL features for schema evolution

sensitivity_points.parameter: Degree of schema change (additive vs. breaking)

tradeoff_points.parameter: Schema evolution strategy (auto-DDL vs. manual migration)
conflicting_attributes: Modifiability (positive: auto-DDL requires no manual intervention)
Security and Compliance (negative: auto-DDL may create uncontrolled schema changes)

scenario_reference.text: A new disparate academic system must be integrated into the centralized DWH using the Spring Framework architecture within 3 weeks without impacting existing integrations

analysis_records.environment: Production DWH with multiple existing integrations running
analysis_records.stimulus: Request to integrate a new legacy academic system
analysis_records.response_measure: Integration completed within 3 weeks with zero impact to existing integrations
architectural_decisions: Modular integration architecture (separate Spring modules per system)
Isolated connection pools per integration
Configuration-driven integration setup
Template-based integration patterns
Independent deployment of new integration modules
analysis_records.reasoning: Spring Framework's modular architecture allows new integration modules to be developed and deployed independently. By using configuration templates and reusable patterns, developers can quickly set up new integrations. Isolated connection pools and separate Spring contexts ensure new integrations don't affect existing ones.

risks.description: Shared DWH database schema contention
risks.rationale: New integration writing to shared DWH tables may cause locking or constraint violations affecting existing integrations

risks.description: Insufficient developer familiarity with Spring Framework
risks.rationale: Per scenario M-03, developers with limited Spring experience may take longer than 3 weeks to implement integration correctly

risks.description: Undocumented legacy system data structures
risks.rationale: If the new legacy system lacks documentation, understanding data structures and relationships may exceed 3-week timeline

non_risks.rationale: Spring's modular architecture supports independent development and deployment of integration modules

sensitivity_points.parameter: Complexity of new legacy system schema

conflicting_attributes: Modifiability - Impact isolation (positive: high isolation prevents impact to existing integrations)
Performance - Resource utilization (negative: high isolation duplicates resources like connection pools)

scenario_reference.text: The DC system must facilitate transfer and storage of binary format data including images, audio, and video files stored on legacy system hard drives

analysis_records.environment: Legacy systems with binary files stored on local file systems
analysis_records.stimulus: Request to synchronize binary data from legacy system to DWH
analysis_records.response_measure: Binary files successfully transferred and stored in DWH without corruption
architectural_decisions: File system integration layer in Spring Framework
Binary data storage strategy (BLOB in database vs. file system with metadata)
Chunked file transfer for large files
Checksum validation for data integrity
Spring Integration File adapters for file system access
analysis_records.reasoning: Spring Integration provides File adapters for reading from file systems. Binary data can be transferred in chunks to avoid memory issues and stored either as BLOBs in the DWH database or in a separate file storage with metadata in the database. Checksum validation ensures data integrity during transfer.

risks.description: Large binary files exceed memory limits
risks.rationale: Video files can be gigabytes in size; loading entire files into memory for transfer may cause OutOfMemory errors

risks.description: File system access permissions on legacy systems
risks.rationale: Spring Framework integration layer may lack necessary file system permissions on legacy system hard drives

risks.description: Network bandwidth saturation from binary transfers
risks.rationale: Transferring large binary files may saturate network bandwidth, impacting other synchronization operations and violating performance scenarios

risks.description: Database BLOB storage performance degradation
risks.rationale: Storing large binary files as BLOBs in the DWH database may degrade query performance per scenario P-05

non_risks.rationale: Spring Integration provides robust file system integration with support for various protocols and chunked transfers

sensitivity_points.parameter: Binary storage strategy (BLOB vs. file system)

tradeoff_points.parameter: Binary data storage location (database BLOB vs. separate file storage)
conflicting_attributes: Compliance - Data centralization (positive: BLOB storage keeps all data in DWH)
Performance - Query performance (negative: BLOBs degrade database query performance per P-05)
Performance - Memory usage (positive: smaller chunks reduce memory footprint)
Performance - Transfer throughput (negative: smaller chunks increase overhead and reduce throughput)

scenario_reference.text: Analytical queries against the centralized DWH must return results within 3 seconds under typical reporting workload to support institutional accreditation processes

analysis_records.environment: Typical reporting workload with multiple concurrent analytical queries
analysis_records.stimulus: User executes analytical query for accreditation reporting
architectural_decisions: Database indexing strategy for common query patterns
Materialized views for pre-aggregated data
Query result caching (Spring Cache with Redis/Ehcache)
Database partitioning for large tables
Read replicas for analytical workloads
analysis_records.reasoning: By implementing appropriate database indexes, materialized views for common aggregations, and query result caching in Spring, most analytical queries can be served within 3 seconds. Database partitioning and read replicas further improve query performance by distributing load and optimizing data access patterns.

risks.description: Ad-hoc queries bypass optimization strategies
risks.rationale: Users may execute complex ad-hoc queries that don't benefit from indexes or materialized views, exceeding 3-second threshold

risks.description: Data volume growth degrades query performance
risks.rationale: As DWH accumulates historical data, query performance may degrade over time even with indexes

risks.description: Cache invalidation complexity with frequent updates
risks.rationale: If master data updates frequently (per P-02), cache invalidation logic becomes complex and may serve stale data

non_risks.rationale: Spring Cache provides proven caching abstraction with support for multiple cache providers

sensitivity_points.correlated_attribute: Performance (query response time and data freshness)

conflicting_attributes: Performance - Query response time (positive: longer TTL increases cache hit rate)
Compliance - Data freshness (negative: longer TTL may serve outdated data for accreditation reports)
Performance - Query response time (positive: pre-aggregated data serves queries faster)
Performance - DWH write load (negative: frequent refresh increases database write operations)
